data.title: Two insurance stocks post strong trading debuts after IPOs
data.description:
American Integrity Insurance (NYSE:AII) stock climbed as much as 19% in its first day of trading after its initial public offering, and Aspen Insurance Holdings (NYSE:AHL) stock gained as much as 12% on Thursday after its debut.

Both show investor appetite for insurers — what's considered a defensive sector — even against elevated volatility in equity markets. Still, the timing was fortunate, with the Nasdaq rising 1.8%, the S&P 500 up 1.4%, and the Dow increased 1.4% in afternoon trading.

Aspen Insurance Holdings (NYSE:AHL) offered 13.2M shares, upsized from its original size of 11M shares, at $30 apiece, late Wednesday. The company, though, received none of the proceeds. Rather, the IPO gave Apollo Global Management (APO) a chance to partially cash out of its investment in the company. After the IPO, Apollo will hold ~85% of AHL shares, assuming the overallotment option is exercised in full. In that case, Apollo would have sold 11.165M of AHL's shares.

American Integrity Insurance (NYSE:AII)  offered 6.875M shares of its common stock at $16 per share. The company sold 6.25M of the shares, and will use net proceeds for purposes, such funding growth at its insurance operations and satisfying tax withholding and remittance obligations associated with certain equity grants. Selling shareholders sold the remaining 625K shares.

Both stocks started trading on the New York Stock Exchange on Thursday.

At 1:05 PM ET, Aspen Insurance (AHL) shares rose 10% to $33.19, and American Integrity (AII) climbed 14% to $18.26.

data.title: Coeur Mining targets $300M free cash flow in 2025 with silver and gold production growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Coeur Mining, Inc. (CDE) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Mitchell Krebs highlighted the company's fourth consecutive quarter of positive EPS and positive free cash flow, supported by higher metal prices and robust performance from Las Chispas and other operations. He emphasized the elimination of nearly $130 million in debt and metal prepaid facilities and stated that Coeur Mining is on track to achieve full-year guidance.

  • Management expects full-year adjusted EBITDA to exceed $700 million and free cash flow to surpass $300 million, projecting a year-end leverage ratio close to zero. The CEO noted that the company has significantly improved its financial stability compared to only a few quarters ago.

  • The integration of Las Chispas was described as smooth, with high-grade production at low costs and continued drilling success in the region. New discoveries at Las Chispas, including the Augusta vein, were highlighted as early examples of exploration progress.

  • CFO Tom Whelan reported revenue of $360 million, adjusted EBITDA of $149 million, net income of $33 million, and free cash flow of $18 million for the quarter. He noted that higher prices and improved operations contributed to a doubling of the EBITDA margin compared to the prior year.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed full-year guidance, expecting silver production growth to drive significant cash flow. The team aims to generate quarterly free cash flow between $75 million and $100 million for the remainder of 2025, supported by elevated gold and silver prices.

  • The company anticipates continued margin expansion, with recovery rates and crusher performance at Rochester expected to improve steadily throughout the year.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for the quarter was $360 million, driven by the addition of Las Chispas and higher average realized prices for gold and silver.

  • Adjusted EBITDA reached $149 million, with a margin of 41%, reflecting a doubling from the prior year. Free cash flow was $18 million, impacted by specific one-time items totaling $130 million.

  • Gold and silver production for the quarter included nearly 90,000 ounces of gold and 4 million ounces of silver, with contributions from all major operations.

  • The balance sheet showed $78 million in cash and $110 million drawn on the revolving credit facility, with management expecting to repay the remaining revolver balance by Q3 2025.

Q&A

  • Wayne Lam, TD Securities, inquired about the expected improvements in silver recoveries at Rochester. CEO Krebs and COO Mick Routledge confirmed that crusher performance is improving and recoveries are expected to trend higher throughout the year as the average crush size decreases.

  • Lam also asked about the stronger-than-expected performance at Wharf. Management attributed this to the timing and grade of material mined, with expectations for consistent performance across the year.

  • Joseph Reagor, ROTH Capital Partners, questioned the integration of Las Chispas and inventory accounting. CFO Whelan explained that the stockpile at Las Chispas is expected to be depleted over the next year, with no significant impacts on free cash flow.

  • Analysts raised questions about cost pressures and labor costs. Management noted stable costs across most operations, with declines in consumable costs balancing out modest increases in labor expenses.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a generally positive outlook, showing interest in the company's deleveraging progress and production growth. Questions focused on operational efficiency and the impact of higher prices on margins.

  • Management's tone was confident during prepared remarks, emphasizing financial stability and strong operational performance. During the Q&A, responses were detailed and focused on operational improvements.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management appeared more optimistic about hitting production and financial targets, driven by the successful integration of Las Chispas and improvements at Rochester.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter's results showed significant improvements in financial metrics compared to Q4 2024, with adjusted EBITDA margins doubling year-over-year.

  • Management's tone was more confident, reflecting improved cash flow projections and operational stability. Analysts' focus shifted from concerns about integration and ramp-up challenges to long-term growth opportunities and financial performance.

  • Strategic priorities have evolved, with a stronger emphasis on deleveraging and maximizing cash flow from existing assets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that crusher performance and recovery rates at Rochester remain key areas to monitor as the year progresses.

  • Analysts raised concerns about potential cost pressures and the impact of weather conditions on operations, particularly at Rochester and Wharf. Management addressed these concerns by highlighting operational flexibility and stable cost trends.

Final Takeaway

Coeur Mining's Q1 2025 results reflect a strong start to the year, with promising operational and financial performance driven by the integration of Las Chispas and improvements at Rochester. Management's proactive debt reduction and focus on achieving full-year guidance signal confidence in the company's ability to deliver record results in 2025. Investors can monitor silver and gold production growth, crusher performance, and cash flow trends as key indicators of continued success.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
